summary: The interview focuses on designing a stock trading app like Robinhood. Jordan, the employee, proposes a three-layer architecture (User, Exchange, Routing/Communication) to handle real-time stock price updates, user positions, and order placement. He deep dives into calculating the National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO) from multiple exchanges using a doubly linked list with a hash map, discusses client-server connections (WebSockets), user layer sharding, and fault tolerance for various components using replication and Zookeeper, ultimately presenting a comprehensive system diagram.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Hey guys, welcome to another system design mock interview. I'm excited about this one because we have the man from one of our favorite channels, it is “Jordan has no life”, and it's Jordan himself. How are you doing, Jordan?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I'm fine, thanks for having me on. Just to give a quick introduction about myself, like Tom mentioned, I run a YouTube channel focused on system design interviews. In addition to that, I also work as a software engineer. I was previously working at Google and now I work at a high-frequency trading company, so hopefully, I have a decent idea of what's going on when it comes to this particular design. Anyway, I'll let it get back to him.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Great to have you on, Jordan. It is a good channel; I know a lot of our subscribers actually requested you to come on here. If you haven't seen Jordan's channel, then do go check it out. All right, should we get straight into the interview? Let's do it. Jordan has seen the question ahead of this. I wanted to try and guarantee that we had a high-quality answer that was as useful as possible for you guys. Jordan, the question I want to ask you today is: how would you design a stock trading app such as Robinhood?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: All right, thanks for letting me get into it. Let's see if I can type at all. Okay, that seems to work. So, like you mentioned, we're designing a stock trading app. I'm going to use Robinhood since that's the one that I know about the most. I'm going to try and lay out ideally the requirements of something like this. The ones that I can think of are, of course, things like sending orders and keeping track of positions that a certain client or customer might actually have. Also, this is the one I want to focus on, so I'm going to put two asterisks here: showing the actual correct price of stock or options. The reason for that being that I think when it comes to actually sending orders and maintaining your customers' actual holdings, that's kind of trivial. You can do that pretty simply with a couple of databases. Maybe you'll have to shard them a little bit if you really do have a ton of users, but for the most part, all the exchanges have an API; you're basically just forwarding their orders there. I think in reality that gets a little bit more complex with payment for order flow and things like that, but we're not going to touch upon that too much ideally. So, my question for you, Tom, then would be: what are the type of capacity estimates we're looking at? Basically, how many users and how many stocks per user were you thinking?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Sure, let's say 100 million users and around 100 stocks per user.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: 100 million and 100 per user. Okay, that gives me a couple of ideas here. We do have 100 million users, so obviously we're talking not just 100 million users, but since we have 100 stocks per user, we're looking at... let's see what comes after a million... a billion... oh my gosh, and then 10 billion after that because it's 100. So we now have 10 billion positions to basically keep track of. That's a lot of data to obviously worry about when multiplying the two of those. A lot of positions. From here, I am going to again talk about my main concerns because, like I mentioned, the part of this problem that I'm really hoping to focus on, if that's all right with you, Tom, is actually showing the correct price of the stock or the option because I think that is the real complexity here. So my main concerns are: I want each client device to have a minimal load because if each client device is connected to too many of our servers at one time, we could be draining the battery, we could be using too much data, and that would be a problem. Also, it would be great if we weren't connecting too many systems to the exchange, limiting our systems with the exchange connectivity. The reason being that you have to pay for that, and if we're Robinhood, we don't want to pay for too much of it. We don't want to be collecting too much data if we can avoid doing it, just keeping it within our system. Do you have any questions about just a general design or anything?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Jordan works at a high-frequency trading company, so obviously has a really strong knowledge of how these kinds of exchanges work. You would not be expected to know all these things; instead, you need to ask the interviewer the right questions in order to understand how the system will need to work. I like that Jordan has already clarified the area of the problem that he hopes to focus on and explains some of the issues that he knows are going to come up over the scope of his design. Perhaps he could have come up with some of the capacity estimates himself, but it's okay that you asked the interviewer instead. Let's get into it. What's the general breakdown of the system that you want to go with?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. I've thought about having three main pieces for the system. I'm going to write them down, but then I'll probably introduce them in reverse order. The first is a user layer; these are going to be servers that talk to our phones/computers. Then I'm also going to have an exchange layer. I think these are going to be the servers that are getting exchange data. Like I mentioned, a really nice thing about splitting these into their own layers is that we can scale them independently, which is good because we want to keep the exchange layer as small as possible, and we want to keep the user layer perhaps as big as many client devices there are to make sure that each client device is just touching one piece of the user layer, but I'll get into that in a bit. Then we've also got this routing layer. The first thing that I am going to try and cover, at least in this video, is the exchange layer. For background, for whoever's watching this, because I think it's important for something like Robinhood, most exchanges are not publishing just "Apple is $80." What they're publishing is that Apple has one offer: someone wants to buy it for $79, someone wants to buy it for $78.5, someone wants to buy it for $78. On the other side of that, someone wants to sell it for $81, someone wants to sell it for $81.3, and someone wants to sell it for, I don't know, $81.6. So the exchange is never actually giving you a price that is going directly to the customer. What we see in Robinhood, which is kind of nice, it simplifies things for us, is one unified price. It's basically this guy right here. As a result of that, what we need to basically do is take the above, hopefully you can see me highlighting stuff, and we need to convert it. Another piece of that which makes this hard is what I just showed above is just one exchange, but in reality, there are a bunch of exchanges that are publishing the same, different data. I think maybe this is less so the case for stocks, I actually can't remember, but for options exchanges, you might have like $78.7 and $78.3 and $78.1 and $81.6. Basically, what this is known as is called the order book. The point is, there's all this data throughout all these different exchanges, and we have to aggregate it somehow in order to provide just one price to our user. That's what we would call the NBBO. The NBBO is basically the best bid, which is the best thing on the left side here, the best price that people are willing to buy for, no one else is willing to sell for, and also the best offer. I think in my mind, what I'd like to do is probably return to users the best bid plus best offer divided by two. Do you think that seems generally reasonable?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, okay, sure. There are many different ways we can show this; we could do like a weighted average or something. But for now, I'm going to be focusing on just providing a really easy evaluation, which is just the best bid plus the best offer divided by two, the average of the two in the order book. The issue now is that we're getting all of this feed data per exchange, and there are a lot of exchanges. So, how are you going to deal with that? There are many exchanges when you're calculating the base price in your publishing layer. What's your approach?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. The issue is we have a lot of tickers, and when I say a ticker, I mean symbols like Apple, Google, or Meta. Those are all the stock names. We've also got a lot of data, so we probably need some amount of sharding. In my head, you need to make sure that all of the data from an individual ticker, regardless of exchange, is going to the same node. So personally, I would like to shard the exchange layer by ticker symbol. Then, within one node, we'll do some processing, and ideally, that processing is going to lead us to getting our NBBO and taking the average of it so that we can give that back to our customers. What I was thinking from there, and let me know if you're okay with me going down this path, is it's not a trivial task to actually get all of the data from these exchanges and quickly republish it. So, would you mind if I did a brief deep dive into the algorithm I was hoping to use in order to do that?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yes, please do.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, let me know if you think I'm going in the right direction, but tentatively, my plan was to have something like this: we know each exchange is publishing data, and on a node, we need to know the best bid and best ask at all times. When a new best bid or best ask comes in from an exchange, it means that we need to invalidate the old one. What I was thinking is actually maintaining a doubly linked list. So, on one given node, let's say just for Apple, I would do the following: I would say Exchange 1 is saying the best bid is $80, Exchange 2 is saying the best bid is $81, and Exchange 3 is saying the best bid is $82. That means the national best bid is $82 because that's how much someone is willing to buy the stock for at the most price. My thinking from there was to basically, we want to be able to get that highest number as quickly as possible, and how I would want to organize these things is in a linked list. Shoot, I can't draw. It would be great if I had my iPad, alas, I don't. So, we make a linked list, but the nice thing about this is that if we ever need to quickly compute the highest value, we'll make sure that our linked list stays sorted so that $82 is always at the top. However, for our linked list to stay sorted, we actually need to be pretty smart about how we organize things. So, I think what I would like to do is use a doubly linked list with a hash map. The nice thing about this hash map is that if I say, "Oh, shoot, a new piece of data from Exchange 2 is coming in," let's say someone bids $83 on Exchange 2, I could see that the node for $81 is here because it's a doubly linked list. I could quickly remove $81 in constant time, so this is O(1) for removes. Then, what I could do from there is... I figured it out, awesome. What I could do from there, after I've done that, is I could then do an O(log n) insert of my new node. In this case, we would say, "Okay, we'll do $83." Why don't we get rid of this guy, binary search the remaining list, and then put $83 where it belongs? So now we've got $83 leads to $82 leads to $80, and we know our national best bid is $83 amongst all of our exchanges, which is great. Then we could do the same exact thing for the offers, but obviously, we want to see the lowest offer, not the highest offer. Then we could just quickly compute the average.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, cool. Jordan is a very good communicator and is giving a textbook performance of how to communicate in system design interviews. He talks through his thought process with me very clearly. He checks in at regular intervals to make sure I'm on board. He's also demonstrating strong technical skills. His knowledge of the exchange has allowed him to already get into complex considerations such as aggregating the large amount of data the exchange puts out via a stateful consumer. These may have some fault tolerance issues down the line, so I'm curious to see how he approaches that. That sounds okay. So, once the base price has been calculated, how are you then going to get it to the user?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. Okay, that's a completely fair question and definitely something that's a bit complex about Robinhood in particular. My general thinking was, and I mentioned this earlier with my concerns, we want to limit the number of connections per client. The reason being that we could have every single one of our publishers, let's say a client has Apple, Google, Meta. The issue is these are all different nodes, right? We're sharding by these. So if a client has 100 stocks, that would mean they would have 100 different connections to publisher nodes, which is problematic for multiple reasons. It's problematic both on the client end because they would have to directly communicate with those publisher nodes, and that would be really bad because you're maintaining a bunch of active server connections, whether that's either long polling or WebSockets or server-sent events. That would either drain the device battery or it would potentially just use a ton of network data. Also, it would be bad for the publisher nodes because they're doing a decent amount of low-latency calculations. It would be really great if we could decouple all of that logic, so that's where I was hoping my user layer would come in. Let me know if you want to ask anything there.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Sorry, how would you want them connected then?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. I kind of alluded to this a second ago that we could be talking about either long polling. Let me bring out the drawing. We've got our phone, great looking phone, nice job, Jordan. We've got our phone, and in addition to that, we've got our user layer. The question is, how do we want these two connected? My personal opinion, I think, because really we have three options: the user layer is constantly going to be publishing values, and so that makes us decide between WebSockets, which is a real-time protocol, server-sent events, and we also have the choice to use long polling, where we basically open an HTTP request on the user layer and then we maintain that for the whole session. That's an option as well. Personally, I think I lean towards WebSockets. The reason being that these are bidirectional, and they also reconnect automatically, and you don't have to send request headers every single time, which is nice. The reason I care about it being bidirectional is that our phone is going to be sending ticker requests to the user layer, and the user layer in turn is going to be responding with the prices of the stock or the option. I think that's pretty important just in the sense that being able to make sure that connection is as fast as possible is going to ensure that we're getting as much data as possible and maintaining a low latency on our phone. Also, just by keeping one connection between a user device ideally and the user layer of our server, I think that would be really, really useful to us. Then, ideally, what I would want to do is, it's important yet again that we actually go ahead and shard our user layer because we have 100 million users, and that's a lot of devices. So, in the ideal case scenario for sharding, we would be horizontally scaling out; we have many different user layer servers. But in the ideal case, all of the users, or rather, one user layer server, would keep a ton of basically all of the common stocks between the users connected to them. For example, if I had User 1 and User 2, and they were connected to the same layer server A, ideally User 1's stocks intersect User 2's stocks would be very high, would be large, because we ideally want to be fetching the same stock prices on the same user layer servers. In practice, though, I think that's just kind of hard to implement, and so for that reason, I think I'm just going to go ahead and propose a pretty simple round-robin load balancing. I'm still thinking about the eventual design that I'm going to draw up here, and I will draw that out in completion probably once I explain my thinking for the original piece. But ideally, I'm going to have a load balancer between the phone and the user layer, and that is probably just going to do some sort of round-robin load balancing to make sure that a certain user layer server isn't getting overloaded by too many clients. Perhaps we could do that round-robin in a weighted way where we consider how many different tickers that user layer server is already fetching because it's possible that we have power users who get a ton of tickers that they're requesting. But for the most part, I think this approach should generally work. Let me know if you have any questions about the sharding on the user layer server.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Interesting. I wanted to ask, what if one of these user servers went down? How can you make sure this piece of the system is fault-tolerant?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure, that's a fair question. We're talking about fault tolerance, which is definitely a tough piece here because we want our system to be redundant in the face of any hardware issues or networking issues. I think it's hopefully pretty clear on a phone when its user layer stops sending updates. What we could do is have our WebSocket connection occasionally send heartbeats, and if our phone stops receiving them, then it's going to want to establish a new connection. I think that's pretty simple for the most part; it can go through the load balancer. It is definitely true that if a user layer server with a lot of users goes down, we could in theory have a Thundering Herd if we were to load balance poorly, where all of our phones try and reconnect to a different user layer server and it overloads it. But that's kind of the point of using round-robin load balancing: that shouldn't happen. So I think that just by making sure to use heartbeats, and if we really wanted to, we could even have a Zookeeper layer which keeps track of the status of each user layer server, and that would help us to confirm that the user layer server is actually down and it's not an issue with the phone or something. But I think using heartbeats ideally should give us a pretty good sense of making sure that all the devices are getting the updates they need.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, Jordan, could you explain to me how the user layer actually connects to the publishing layer?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. Like I mentioned, I wanted to talk about three pieces here, and I've spoken about two of them right now, and then I'll draw it all out at the end. We've got our user layer, our exchange layer, and the third piece is this routing layer. That is what I will now talk about. My thinking for the routing layer was, in theory, you have, let's say, X user servers and Y publisher servers, or exchange servers, let's call them that. What we know is that every single user server is probably connecting, or at least a user server probably wants at least 100 tickers, right? Because we know it's connected to at least one user. It's probably got many users. In reality, we could be looking at thousands of tickers. You may want thousands of tickers or integrity data cases. That's going to be problematic, especially potentially for the publisher server. If nothing else, I mean, the user server, it may be problematic too, but that's what we have the sharding for. For the publishing server, though, the issue is, let's say out of our 100 million users, every single one of them owns Apple, or maybe 10% of them own Apple. We could potentially have 10 million devices or 10 million user servers or something asking for Apple. If that happens, that could bog down our Apple publisher. So I would prefer that not happen and ideally continue to make it so that all of our different layers of our service can scale independently. So what I would like to do in this type of case is add an intermediary routing or communication layer, whatever you want to call it. I was thinking about how I would like to do this, and I think a lot of people might end up just going with a message broker here. I think something like Kafka does make sense specifically because it's a log-based message broker, so all of the pieces of data that we publish would be persistent on disk, which might be nice for future logging or diagnosis or metrics or anything like that. Also, it would be nice just because then you only have to publish to one place, and many different consumers can get the message. But truthfully, the more I thought about it, I actually think that maybe a better solution would be to use something like a Redis cache. Again, we can scale these caches independently and use a load balancer as a Zookeeper cluster to make sure of which cache is maintaining the data for which tickers. But all this Redis cache is going to hold is actually going to be the option valuation or the most recent one. So what it'll do is, a publisher, say, "Publisher Tesla," says, "Hey, the price for Tesla is now $450," after listening to the exchange. So exchange data comes in, goes to the publisher who publishes $450, who goes to the routing communication layer, which again is nice because we can scale, replicate, and do anything we need independently there. So that's going to be the routing communication layer for Tesla, which is now going to update its value. Maybe it was $445 before, and now it's updated to $450. Then, yet again, what you could do is use something like a server-sent event or even a long poll, and that's going to go to our user servers, which then finally end up going to our client device. Let me know if you think that's okay, and then hopefully we can almost get to the full-on drawing of the design.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, yeah. I did have another question I wanted to ask about: what can we do if a publisher goes down?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure, that's really fair, and that's probably the other big piece of fault tolerance: making sure that our intermediary routing/communication layer and our actual publishing layer are also fault-tolerant. For the routing/communication layer, I think it's just a matter of using replication. It's no big deal if each publisher is publishing to three places instead of one; it's a big deal if our publisher is publishing to a thousand places instead of one. So I think using replication, 3x or something like that, is totally fine. As for the publishing layer, the interesting thing is again, my solution is going to be replication. However, the interesting thing to note is that our publishers are stateful. So it's very important that every single publisher, or rather the backup for a given publisher, is also receiving the same messages that the primary publisher is. Otherwise, what we would see is one publisher goes down, and then the other one is going to have to take a little bit to warm up and get data from the exchanges before it can actually start publishing out stock and option valuations. So I think what that means to me is that personally, I think we should use something called state machine replication here, where rather than doing our typical form of replication where one publisher would send all of its state to the next, what I think we could actually do is have our backup publishers basically listening to the exchange and, ideally, by listening to the exchange, getting the same type of information locally that the primary publisher would have. Then, for example, if our communication layer receives no update/heartbeat from our publishing layer/exchange layer, then we go to Zookeeper, we say, "Do we think this thing is down?" If it is down, then we switch to our backup. Does that seem okay?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, okay, cool. So that makes sense. Jordan's almost 30 minutes into the interview, and he hasn't yet started drawing. We normally recommend that you want to be drawing your diagram after 20 minutes or earlier. Jordan's taking another approach; he's first talking through the various considerations, like a good teacher, and as an interviewer, I encourage this by asking lots of questions. That means he now has a great foundation on which to draw his diagram, but another interviewer might have wanted to see some design mapping earlier. Yes, please go ahead. I was hoping to, we've kind of done all this background now, and I think what I'm hoping to do from here is draw this out all in one aggregated diagram to really tie it all together. Then, if you have any questions from there, feel free to ask me more. But hopefully, this really ties things together now that we've done a lot of abstract talking.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So I've got what I'll consider to be my client device over here, a phone. Very simple. That phone is going to be showing things like Apple is currently $81, Google is currently $200, and Meta is $21. Okay, so we've got our phone right here, seems simple enough. How do we make sure that these prices are actually getting to it? Like I mentioned, the first aspect of it is that we want to establish some sort of WebSocket connection. I'm not going to use a direct arrow here because these are bidirectional. The first thing is that it would have to effectively request to get a WebSocket in the first place. So what we would do is request some load balancer, I think. I mentioned I wanted to do this in a round-robin way so that our user layer isn't handling too much. But we've got our load balancer, and then ideally what this is going to do is maintain a connection with just one user server, where from then on, we can go ahead and perform more requests. So, here would be an example of User Server 1, but we're horizontally scaling this out, so we might have a User Server X over here that we're not connected to in this particular case. So for this guy, this is going to be a WebSocket connection where one of the types of things that you might do is say, "Give me the price of Apple," and then in response down the WebSocket chain, you would say, "Apple is, I don't know, $80." So that would be the example of the user layer, and ideally, we could scale this out independently. Now, in addition to that, we've kind of started building out our user layer. My next goal ideally is going to be to build out our communication layer. Again, I mentioned these were like a bunch of different Redis instances. Imagine that we have replicas and partitions right here, but I'm going to just write this out freehand: communication layer. I think it doesn't have to be Redis, but the reason I like that in particular is it just keeps things relatively fast. All writes and reads are coming in and out of memory. It's effectively a cache sharded by ticker or some combination of tickers, and it maintains connections for us. Cool. Then so that is effectively going to be publishing out to our user servers. The nice thing about this is this is kind of managing all of our network connections, so managing overhead of networking. Ideally, perhaps this is a little bit over-engineered, but I'm going to go with it for now. Then the last thing that we've got, of course, is going to be our publishers, which are listening to the exchange, keeping data in memory. So this is going to be like Tesla primary, and then we've also got Tesla secondary, and then we've got our Apple primary, and we've got our Apple secondary. These guys, like I mentioned, are all just listening to a UDP feed which is coming from many, many different exchanges. Exchange 1, because for example, for options, what I understand, there are 16 or 17 different exchanges that you have to be aware of. Exchange 2, and also Exchange 3. All of these guys are in turn going to be publishing to just about every single one of our exchange layers right here. So it's important to recognize that we're able to aggregate these results into not only just an NBBO but then one price that we can send to the rest of our application. Hopefully, you're starting to get the point at this point, but everyone is receiving all of the data. Okay. Then from there, in turn, we've got probably we need some sort of coordination service like a Zookeeper, which is going to keep track of all of our partitions. It's going to be connected to everything, has a sense of what tickers are located where. Then, of course, we've got just our primaries at the moment publishing over here to our communication layer. Then the last piece of this, because even though I called it trivial, I should still probably write it down, is actually going to be an orders/position service. For this one in particular, I think there probably would still be a load balancer in between them, and ideally, what we would want to do with this load balancer is use something like consistent hashing. The reason being that if someone is constantly trying to request what stocks or positions they're holding, it's probably not changed. So it'd be great if we could actually cache that on our orders and position service. For example, if this guy was split into three different servers, it would be great if for my particular user, I was always going to this server right here because then the result of my query would already be cached. But in the background, I would imagine that for your orders and position service, there are basically two endpoints: one of which is taking your order to the exchange to get a position, and then upon receiving a successful response to that, is probably going to go ahead and write to some sort of database. I personally think MySQL is fine here. I just think there aren't that many interactions with this; people on a Robinhood app are not trading that frequently that I think your database is going to be your bottleneck. I think it's more significant to figure out the challenge of providing base prices. I think something like just sharding by user ID and then having a positions table which, for every single given user, has a row like user ID and stock ID, and maybe some metadata like the price they bought it at or something. But truthfully, that's kind of semantics from there. So this is kind of the design I'm going to stick with and perhaps double down upon. Let me know if you have any questions about it.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, yeah, interesting. I think it looks like a good design to me. I'm coming to the end of the interview, so we're going to have to wrap it up. But before we do, is there anything you'd like to just take a minute to look at? Is there anything you'd like to refine or add to the design?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: For sure. Considering we don't have too much time left, I'm not really sure how much I can refine at this point. But I definitely think if there's one area I'd like to think about a little bit more, it would probably be this communication layer. I'm pretty confident that Redis is a decently good technology to use here just because we really only care about the most recent data point. We don't need a full queue if, for some reason, a consumer of the data is delayed; it's not important to get old data. Though I do just wonder what the best way to scale this thing out is, ensure fault tolerance, and make sure that we're horizontally scaling out each layer separately, independently, in a good way that minimizes the amount of resources that we're using, keeps our costs down. So, nothing specific to necessarily change about this part, but I would probably just want to think it through a little bit more to ultimately make sure that I'm making this system as flexible as possible. But I think that's about it.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, well, let's then end the interview there. I was happy with Jordan's design and decided to wrap up the interview. But another interviewer might have spent a few more minutes with Jordan to dive a bit deeper into certain aspects. Perhaps I could have grilled him a bit more on the fault tolerance of the system because even though his approach there seemed to be generally good, it might have been interesting to look at it in a bit more detail. In any case, Jordan made a smart choice in breaking a system into three different layers to scale independently while also considering the load that it would have on client devices. Overall, a really strong design and a very impressive interview from Jordan. Great job! That's great, thanks for doing that. How was it? Did you have fun? Of course, always a great time making systems, so no complaints, no issues there. Great, well, thanks for coming on, and I hope we can have you back on here one day because I really enjoyed it, and I think everyone will get a lot out of that. So, thanks a lot.

training_pairs.question: How would you design a stock trading app such as Robinhood?
training_pairs.answer: All right, thanks for letting me get into it. Let's see if I can type at all. Okay, that seems to work. So, like you mentioned, we're designing a stock trading app. I'm going to use Robinhood since that's the one that I know about the most. I'm going to try and lay out ideally the requirements of something like this. The ones that I can think of are, of course, things like sending orders and keeping track of positions that a certain client or customer might actually have. Also, this is the one I want to focus on, so I'm going to put two asterisks here: showing the actual correct price of stock or options. The reason for that being that I think when it comes to actually sending orders and maintaining your customers' actual holdings, that's kind of trivial. You can do that pretty simply with a couple of databases. Maybe you'll have to shard them a little bit if you really do have a ton of users, but for the most part, all the exchanges have an API; you're basically just forwarding their orders there. I think in reality that gets a little bit more complex with payment for order flow and things like that, but we're not going to touch upon that too much ideally. So, my question for you, Tom, then would be: what are the type of capacity estimates we're looking at? Basically, how many users and how many stocks per user were you thinking?
training_pairs.follow_up: What are the type of capacity estimates we're looking at? Basically, how many users and how many stocks per user were you thinking?

training_pairs.question: Sure, let's say 100 million users and around 100 stocks per user.
training_pairs.answer: 100 million and 100 per user. Okay, that gives me a couple of ideas here. We do have 100 million users, so obviously we're talking not just 100 million users, but since we have 100 stocks per user, we're looking at... let's see what comes after a million... a billion... oh my gosh, and then 10 billion after that because it's 100. So we now have 10 billion positions to basically keep track of. That's a lot of data to obviously worry about when multiplying the two of those. A lot of positions. From here, I am going to again talk about my main concerns because, like I mentioned, the part of this problem that I'm really hoping to focus on, if that's all right with you, Tom, is actually showing the correct price of the stock or the option because I think that is the real complexity here. So my main concerns are: I want each client device to have a minimal load because if each client device is connected to too many of our servers at one time, we could be draining the battery, we could be using too much data, and that would be a problem. Also, it would be great if we weren't connecting too many systems to the exchange, limiting our systems with the exchange connectivity. The reason being that you have to pay for that, and if we're Robinhood, we don't want to pay for too much of it. We don't want to be collecting too much data if we can avoid doing it, just keeping it within our system. Do you have any questions about just a general design or anything?
training_pairs.follow_up: What's the general breakdown of the system that you want to go with?

training_pairs.question: What's the general breakdown of the system that you want to go with?
training_pairs.answer: Sure. I've thought about having three main pieces for the system. I'm going to write them down, but then I'll probably introduce them in reverse order. The first is a user layer; these are going to be servers that talk to our phones/computers. Then I'm also going to have an exchange layer. I think these are going to be the servers that are getting exchange data. Like I mentioned, a really nice thing about splitting these into their own layers is that we can scale them independently, which is good because we want to keep the exchange layer as small as possible, and we want to keep the user layer perhaps as big as many client devices there are to make sure that each client device is just touching one piece of the user layer, but I'll get into that in a bit. Then we've also got this routing layer. The first thing that I am going to try and cover, at least in this video, is the exchange layer. For background, for whoever's watching this, because I think it's important for something like Robinhood, most exchanges are not publishing just "Apple is $80." What they're publishing is that Apple has one offer: someone wants to buy it for $79, someone wants to buy it for $78.5, someone wants to buy it for $78. On the other side of that, someone wants to sell it for $81, someone wants to sell it for $81.3, and someone wants to sell it for, I don't know, $81.6. So the exchange is never actually giving you a price that is going directly to the customer. What we see in Robinhood, which is kind of nice, it simplifies things for us, is one unified price. It's basically this guy right here. As a result of that, what we need to basically do is take the above, hopefully you can see me highlighting stuff, and we need to convert it. Another piece of that which makes this hard is what I just showed above is just one exchange, but in reality, there are a bunch of exchanges that are publishing the same, different data. I think maybe this is less so the case for stocks, I actually can't remember, but for options exchanges, you might have like $78.7 and $78.3 and $78.1 and $81.6. Basically, what this is known as is called the order book. The point is, there's all this data throughout all these different exchanges, and we have to aggregate it somehow in order to provide just one price to our user. That's what we would call the NBBO. The NBBO is basically the best bid, which is the best thing on the left side here, the best price that people are willing to buy for, no one else is willing to sell for, and also the best offer. I think in my mind, what I'd like to do is probably return to users the best bid plus best offer divided by two. Do you think that seems generally reasonable?
training_pairs.follow_up: How are you going to deal with that? There are many exchanges when you're calculating the base price in your publishing layer. What's your approach?

training_pairs.question: How are you going to deal with that? There are many exchanges when you're calculating the base price in your publishing layer. What's your approach?
training_pairs.answer: Sure. The issue is we have a lot of tickers, and when I say a ticker, I mean symbols like Apple, Google, or Meta. Those are all the stock names. We've also got a lot of data, so we probably need some amount of sharding. In my head, you need to make sure that all of the data from an individual ticker, regardless of exchange, is going to the same node. So personally, I would like to shard the exchange layer by ticker symbol. Then, within one node, we'll do some processing, and ideally, that processing is going to lead us to getting our NBBO and taking the average of it so that we can give that back to our customers. What I was thinking from there, and let me know if you're okay with me going down this path, is it's not a trivial task to actually get all of the data from these exchanges and quickly republish it. So, would you mind if I did a brief deep dive into the algorithm I was hoping to use in order to do that?

training_pairs.question: Yes, please do.
training_pairs.answer: Okay, let me know if you think I'm going in the right direction, but tentatively, my plan was to have something like this: we know each exchange is publishing data, and on a node, we need to know the best bid and best ask at all times. When a new best bid or best ask comes in from an exchange, it means that we need to invalidate the old one. What I was thinking is actually maintaining a doubly linked list. So, on one given node, let's say just for Apple, I would do the following: I would say Exchange 1 is saying the best bid is $80, Exchange 2 is saying the best bid is $81, and Exchange 3 is saying the best bid is $82. That means the national best bid is $82 because that's how much someone is willing to buy the stock for at the most price. My thinking from there was to basically, we want to be able to get that highest number as quickly as possible, and how I would want to organize these things is in a linked list. Shoot, I can't draw. It would be great if I had my iPad, alas, I don't. So, we make a linked list, but the nice thing about this is that if we ever need to quickly compute the highest value, we'll make sure that our linked list stays sorted so that $82 is always at the top. However, for our linked list to stay sorted, we actually need to be pretty smart about how we organize things. So, I think what I would like to do is use a doubly linked list with a hash map. The nice thing about this hash map is that if I say, "Oh, shoot, a new piece of data from Exchange 2 is coming in," let's say someone bids $83 on Exchange 2, I could see that the node for $81 is here because it's a doubly linked list. I could quickly remove $81 in constant time, so this is O(1) for removes. Then, what I could do from there is... I figured it out, awesome. What I could do from there, after I've done that, is I could then do an O(log n) insert of my new node. In this case, we would say, "Okay, we'll do $83." Why don't we get rid of this guy, binary search the remaining list, and then put $83 where it belongs? So now we've got $83 leads to $82 leads to $80, and we know our national best bid is $83 amongst all of our exchanges, which is great. Then we could do the same exact thing for the offers, but obviously, we want to see the lowest offer, not the highest offer. Then we could just quickly compute the average.
training_pairs.follow_up: Once the base price has been calculated, how are you then going to get it to the user?

training_pairs.question: Once the base price has been calculated, how are you then going to get it to the user?
training_pairs.answer: Sure. Okay, that's a completely fair question and definitely something that's a bit complex about Robinhood in particular. My general thinking was, and I mentioned this earlier with my concerns, we want to limit the number of connections per client. The reason being that we could have every single one of our publishers, let's say a client has Apple, Google, Meta. The issue is these are all different nodes, right? We're sharding by these. So if a client has 100 stocks, that would mean they would have 100 different connections to publisher nodes, which is problematic for multiple reasons. It's problematic both on the client end because they would have to directly communicate with those publisher nodes, and that would be really bad because you're maintaining a bunch of active server connections, whether that's either long polling or WebSockets or server-sent events. That would either drain the device battery or it would potentially just use a ton of network data. Also, it would be bad for the publisher nodes because they're doing a decent amount of low-latency calculations. It would be really great if we could decouple all of that logic, so that's where I was hoping my user layer would come in. Let me know if you want to ask anything there.
training_pairs.follow_up: How would you want them connected then?

training_pairs.question: How would you want them connected then?
training_pairs.answer: Sure. I kind of alluded to this a second ago that we could be talking about either long polling. Let me bring out the drawing. We've got our phone, great looking phone, nice job, Jordan. We've got our phone, and in addition to that, we've got our user layer. The question is, how do we want these two connected? My personal opinion, I think, because really we have three options: the user layer is constantly going to be publishing values, and so that makes us decide between WebSockets, which is a real-time protocol, server-sent events, and we also have the choice to use long polling, where we basically open an HTTP request on the user layer and then we maintain that for the whole session. That's an option as well. Personally, I think I lean towards WebSockets. The reason being that these are bidirectional, and they also reconnect automatically, and you don't have to send request headers every single time, which is nice. The reason I care about it being bidirectional is that our phone is going to be sending ticker requests to the user layer, and the user layer in turn is going to be responding with the prices of the stock or the option. I think that's pretty important just in the sense that being able to make sure that connection is as fast as possible is going to ensure that we're getting as much data as possible and maintaining a low latency on our phone. Also, just by keeping one connection between a user device ideally and the user layer of our server, I think that would be really, really useful to us. Then, ideally, what I would want to do is, it's important yet again that we actually go ahead and shard our user layer because we have 100 million users, and that's a lot of devices. So, in the ideal case scenario for sharding, we would be horizontally scaling out; we have many different user layer servers. But in the ideal case, all of the users, or rather, one user layer server, would keep a ton of basically all of the common stocks between the users connected to them. For example, if I had User 1 and User 2, and they were connected to the same layer server A, ideally User 1's stocks intersect User 2's stocks would be very high, would be large, because we ideally want to be fetching the same stock prices on the same user layer servers. In practice, though, I think that's just kind of hard to implement, and so for that reason, I think I'm just going to go ahead and propose a pretty simple round-robin load balancing. I'm still thinking about the eventual design that I'm going to draw up here, and I will draw that out in completion probably once I explain my thinking for the original piece. But ideally, I'm going to have a load balancer between the phone and the user layer, and that is probably just going to do some sort of round-robin load balancing to make sure that a certain user layer server isn't getting overloaded by too many clients. Perhaps we could do that round-robin in a weighted way where we consider how many different tickers that user layer server is already fetching because it's possible that we have power users who get a ton of tickers that they're requesting. But for the most part, I think this approach should generally work. Let me know if you have any questions about the sharding on the user layer server.
training_pairs.follow_up: What if one of these user servers went down? How can you make sure this piece of the system is fault-tolerant?

training_pairs.question: What if one of these user servers went down? How can you make sure this piece of the system is fault-tolerant?
training_pairs.answer: Sure, that's a fair question. We're talking about fault tolerance, which is definitely a tough piece here because we want our system to be redundant in the face of any hardware issues or networking issues. I think it's hopefully pretty clear on a phone when its user layer stops sending updates. What we could do is have our WebSocket connection occasionally send heartbeats, and if our phone stops receiving them, then it's going to want to establish a new connection. I think that's pretty simple for the most part; it can go through the load balancer. It is definitely true that if a user layer server with a lot of users goes down, we could in theory have a Thundering Herd if we were to load balance poorly, where all of our phones try and reconnect to a different user layer server and it overloads it. But that's kind of the point of using round-robin load balancing: that shouldn't happen. So I think that just by making sure to use heartbeats, and if we really wanted to, we could even have a Zookeeper layer which keeps track of the status of each user layer server, and that would help us to confirm that the user layer server is actually down and it's not an issue with the phone or something. But I think using heartbeats ideally should give us a pretty good sense of making sure that all the devices are getting the updates they need.
training_pairs.follow_up: Jordan, could you explain to me how the user layer actually connects to the publishing layer?

training_pairs.question: Jordan, could you explain to me how the user layer actually connects to the publishing layer?
training_pairs.answer: Sure. Like I mentioned, I wanted to talk about three pieces here, and I've spoken about two of them right now, and then I'll draw it all out at the end. We've got our user layer, our exchange layer, and the third piece is this routing layer. That is what I will now talk about. My thinking for the routing layer was, in theory, you have, let's say, X user servers and Y publisher servers, or exchange servers, let's call them that. What we know is that every single user server is probably connecting, or at least a user server probably wants at least 100 tickers, right? Because we know it's connected to at least one user. It's probably got many users. In reality, we could be looking at thousands of tickers. You may want thousands of tickers or integrity data cases. That's going to be problematic, especially potentially for the publisher server. If nothing else, I mean, the user server, it may be problematic too, but that's what we have the sharding for. For the publishing server, though, the issue is, let's say out of our 100 million users, every single one of them owns Apple, or maybe 10% of them own Apple. We could potentially have 10 million devices or 10 million user servers or something asking for Apple. If that happens, that could bog down our Apple publisher. So I would prefer that not happen and ideally continue to make it so that all of our different layers of our service can scale independently. So what I would like to do in this type of case is add an intermediary routing or communication layer, whatever you want to call it. I was thinking about how I would like to do this, and I think a lot of people might end up just going with a message broker here. I think something like Kafka does make sense specifically because it's a log-based message broker, so all of the pieces of data that we publish would be persistent on disk, which might be nice for future logging or diagnosis or metrics or anything like that. Also, it would be nice just because then you only have to publish to one place, and many different consumers can get the message. But truthfully, the more I thought about it, I actually think that maybe a better solution would be to use something like a Redis cache. Again, we can scale these caches independently and use a load balancer as a Zookeeper cluster to make sure of which cache is maintaining the data for which tickers. But all this Redis cache is going to hold is actually going to be the option valuation or the most recent one. So what it'll do is, a publisher, say, "Publisher Tesla," says, "Hey, the price for Tesla is now $450," after listening to the exchange. So exchange data comes in, goes to the publisher who publishes $450, who goes to the routing communication layer, which again is nice because we can scale, replicate, and do anything we need independently there. So that's going to be the routing communication layer for Tesla, which is now going to update its value. Maybe it was $445 before, and now it's updated to $450. Then, yet again, what you could do is use something like a server-sent event or even a long poll, and that's going to go to our user servers, which then finally end up going to our client device. Let me know if you think that's okay, and then hopefully we can almost get to the full-on drawing of the design.
training_pairs.follow_up: What can we do if a publisher goes down?

training_pairs.question: What can we do if a publisher goes down?
training_pairs.answer: Sure, that's really fair, and that's probably the other big piece of fault tolerance: making sure that our intermediary routing/communication layer and our actual publishing layer are also fault-tolerant. For the routing/communication layer, I think it's just a matter of using replication. It's no big deal if each publisher is publishing to three places instead of one; it's a big deal if our publisher is publishing to a thousand places instead of one. So I think using replication, 3x or something like that, is totally fine. As for the publishing layer, the interesting thing is again, my solution is going to be replication. However, the interesting thing to note is that our publishers are stateful. So it's very important that every single publisher, or rather the backup for a given publisher, is also receiving the same messages that the primary publisher is. Otherwise, what we would see is one publisher goes down, and then the other one is going to have to take a little bit to warm up and get data from the exchanges before it can actually start publishing out stock and option valuations. So I think what that means to me is that personally, I think we should use something called state machine replication here, where rather than doing our typical form of replication where one publisher would send all of its state to the next, what I think we could actually do is have our backup publishers basically listening to the exchange and, ideally, by listening to the exchange, getting the same type of information locally that the primary publisher would have. Then, for example, if our communication layer receives no update/heartbeat from our publishing layer/exchange layer, then we go to Zookeeper, we say, "Do we think this thing is down?" If it is down, then we switch to our backup. Does that seem okay?

training_pairs.question: Yes, please go ahead. I was hoping to, we've kind of done all this background now, and I think what I'm hoping to do from here is draw this out all in one aggregated diagram to really tie it all together. Then, if you have any questions from there, feel free to ask me more. But hopefully, this really ties things together now that we've done a lot of abstract talking.
training_pairs.answer: So I've got what I'll consider to be my client device over here, a phone. Very simple. That phone is going to be showing things like Apple is currently $81, Google is currently $200, and Meta is $21. Okay, so we've got our phone right here, seems simple enough. How do we make sure that these prices are actually getting to it? Like I mentioned, the first aspect of it is that we want to establish some sort of WebSocket connection. I'm not going to use a direct arrow here because these are bidirectional. The first thing is that it would have to effectively request to get a WebSocket in the first place. So what we would do is request some load balancer, I think. I mentioned I wanted to do this in a round-robin way so that our user layer isn't handling too much. But we've got our load balancer, and then ideally what this is going to do is maintain a connection with just one user server, where from then on, we can go ahead and perform more requests. So, here would be an example of User Server 1, but we're horizontally scaling this out, so we might have a User Server X over here that we're not connected to in this particular case. So for this guy, this is going to be a WebSocket connection where one of the types of things that you might do is say, "Give me the price of Apple," and then in response down the WebSocket chain, you would say, "Apple is, I don't know, $80." So that would be the example of the user layer, and ideally, we could scale this out independently. Now, in addition to that, we've kind of started building out our user layer. My next goal ideally is going to be to build out our communication layer. Again, I mentioned these were like a bunch of different Redis instances. Imagine that we have replicas and partitions right here, but I'm going to just write this out freehand: communication layer. I think it doesn't have to be Redis, but the reason I like that in particular is it just keeps things relatively fast. All writes and reads are coming in and out of memory. It's effectively a cache sharded by ticker or some combination of tickers, and it maintains connections for us. Cool. Then so that is effectively going to be publishing out to our user servers. The nice thing about this is this is kind of managing all of our network connections, so managing overhead of networking. Ideally, perhaps this is a little bit over-engineered, but I'm going to go with it for now. Then the last thing that we've got, of course, is going to be our publishers, which are listening to the exchange, keeping data in memory. So this is going to be like Tesla primary, and then we've also got Tesla secondary, and then we've got our Apple primary, and we've got our Apple secondary. These guys, like I mentioned, are all just listening to a UDP feed which is coming from many, many different exchanges. Exchange 1, because for example, for options, what I understand, there are 16 or 17 different exchanges that you have to be aware of. Exchange 2, and also Exchange 3. All of these guys are in turn going to be publishing to just about every single one of our exchange layers right here. So it's important to recognize that we're able to aggregate these results into not only just an NBBO but then one price that we can send to the rest of our application. Hopefully, you're starting to get the point at this point, but everyone is receiving all of the data. Okay. Then from there, in turn, we've got probably we need some sort of coordination service like a Zookeeper, which is going to keep track of all of our partitions. It's going to be connected to everything, has a sense of what tickers are located where. Then, of course, we've got just our primaries at the moment publishing over here to our communication layer. Then the last piece of this, because even though I called it trivial, I should still probably write it down, is actually going to be an orders/position service. For this one in particular, I think there probably would still be a load balancer in between them, and ideally, what we would want to do with this load balancer is use something like consistent hashing. The reason being that if someone is constantly trying to request what stocks or positions they're holding, it's probably not changed. So it'd be great if we could actually cache that on our orders and position service. For example, if this guy was split into three different servers, it would be great if for my particular user, I was always going to this server right here because then the result of my query would already be cached. But in the background, I would imagine that for your orders and position service, there are basically two endpoints: one of which is taking your order to the exchange to get a position, and then upon receiving a successful response to that, is probably going to go ahead and write to some sort of database. I personally think MySQL is fine here. I just think there aren't that many interactions with this; people on a Robinhood app are not trading that frequently that I think your database is going to be your bottleneck. I think it's more significant to figure out the challenge of providing base prices. I think something like just sharding by user ID and then having a positions table which, for every single given user, has a row like user ID and stock ID, and maybe some metadata like the price they bought it at or something. But truthfully, that's kind of semantics from there. So this is kind of the design I'm going to stick with and perhaps double down upon. Let me know if you have any questions about it.
training_pairs.follow_up: Is there anything you'd like to refine or add to the design?

training_pairs.question: Is there anything you'd like to refine or add to the design?
training_pairs.answer: For sure. Considering we don't have too much time left, I'm not really sure how much I can refine at this point. But I definitely think if there's one area I'd like to think about a little bit more, it would probably be this communication layer. I'm pretty confident that Redis is a decently good technology to use here just because we really only care about the most recent data point. We don't need a full queue if, for some reason, a consumer of the data is delayed; it's not important to get old data. Though I do just wonder what the best way to scale this thing out is, ensure fault tolerance, and make sure that we're horizontally scaling out each layer separately, independently, in a good way that minimizes the amount of resources that we're using, keeps our costs down. So, nothing specific to necessarily change about this part, but I would probably just want to think it through a little bit more to ultimately make sure that I'm making this system as flexible as possible. But I think that's about it.
